steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a punctuation question. The blank falls between the festival name and a non-essential appositive phrase (“or FESTAC ’77 … known”) that is already closed by a dash. The skill is dash enclosure: a non-essential element set off by one dash requires a matching dash on the other side.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Culture:’: A colon must follow an independent clause; “the Second World Black and African Festival of Arts and Culture” is not an independent clause, so the colon is ungrammatical. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘Culture—’: An opening dash here matches the closing dash after “known,” correctly enclosing the non-essential appositive on both sides. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘Culture,’: A non-essential element must be set off with matching punctuation. Because the closing punctuation is a dash, the opening punctuation cannot be a comma. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘Culture’: No punctuation before the appositive leaves the phrase unset-off, violating the rule that non-essential elements require enclosing punctuation. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
